1: Psalm 99 is the third psalm to open with YHWH mālāk. יְהוָה מָלָךְ יִרְגְּזוּ עַמִּים יֹשֵׁב כְּרוּבִים תָּנוּט הָאָרֶץ (yĕhwāh mālāk yirgĕzû ʿammîm yōshêb kĕrûbîm tānûṭ hāʾāreṣ, "The LORD reigns; let the peoples tremble! He is enthroned above the cherubim; let the earth shake!") — yirgĕzû: to tremble, to be agitated (Psalms 77:16,18 — the shaking of the earth before God). Yōshêb kĕrûbîm: the one who sits/is enthroned between the cherubim — the ark-presence formula (1 Samuel 4:4; 2 Samuel 6:2; Psalms 80:1). The cherubim were the golden figures atop the ark over which the divine glory hovered. Tānûṭ hāʾāreṣ: let the earth shake/totter (the same verb for the earth reeling — Psalms 46:2-3; Isaiah 24:20).
2: יְהוָה בְּצִיּוֹן גָּדוֹל וְרָם הוּא עַל-כָּל-הָעַמִּים (yĕhwāh bĕṣiyyôn gādôl wĕrām hûʾ ʿal-kol-hāʿammîm, "The LORD is great in Zion; he is raised high above all the peoples!") — the universal kingship (v1: yirgĕzû ʿammîm) grounded in the particular place (Zion): God reigns from Zion over all peoples. Gādôl: great (in all attributes); rām: high/exalted (the spatial metaphor for supremacy — Isaiah 2:11,17; Ezekiel 17:24). The movement from Zion-particularity to universal height is characteristic of the enthronement psalms.
3: יוֹדוּ שִׁמְךָ גָּדוֹל וְנוֹרָא קָדוֹשׁ הוּא (yôdû shimĕkā gādôl wĕnôrāʾ qādôsh hûʾ, "Let them praise your name — great and awesome! Holy is he!") — the first occurrence of the psalm's triple refrain: qādôsh hûʾ ("holy is he" — vv3, 5, 9). Each refrain concludes a section and is the theological hinge on which everything turns. Qādôsh: the holy (the word denoting the absolute otherness of God — his being set apart from all that is creaturely and sinful). The Seraphim's cry of Isaiah 6:3 ("holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts") is the heavenly parallel to this triple refrain. Hûʾ: he — the pronoun carries weight: it is this one, the LORD of Zion (v2), who is holy.
4: וְעֹז מֶלֶךְ מִשְׁפָּט אָהֵב אַתָּה כּוֹנַנְתָּ מֵישָׁרִים (wĕʿōz melek mishpāṭ ʾāhêb ʾattāh kônantā mêshārîm, "The mighty King loves justice! You have established equity!") — ʿōz melek: the king's strength/might (the power that belongs to the king — Psalms 29:11; Psalms 46:1). Mishpāṭ ʾāhêb: he loves justice — not merely performs it, but loves it (Isaiah 61:8: "I the LORD love justice"; Micah 6:8). Kônantā mêshārîm: you have established what is level/equitable (kônen: to set up firmly; mêshārîm: equities — Psalms 45:6; Psalms 98:9). מִשְׁפָּט וּצְדָקָה בְּיַעֲקֹב אַתָּה עָשִׂיתָ (mishpāṭ ûṣĕdāqāh bĕyaʿăqōb ʾattāh ʿāśîtā, "justice and righteousness in Jacob you have carried out!") — the universal king's work made concrete in Jacob/Israel.
5: רוֹמְמוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ וְהִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לַהֲדֹם רַגְלָיו קָדוֹשׁ הוּא (rômĕmû yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhênû wĕhishtaḥăwû lahădom raglāyw qādôsh hûʾ, "Exalt the LORD our God and bow down at his footstool — holy is he!") — the second refrain (qādôsh hûʾ). Rômĕmû (polel: to lift up high — exalt to the highest point); hishtaḥăwû: prostrate yourselves (complete bodily submission). Lahădom raglāyw: at his footstool (Isaiah 66:1: "heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool"; 1 Chronicles 28:2: the ark described as God's footstool; Psalms 110:1: the enemies made a footstool). The posture of prostration at the footstool combines the highest exaltation of God with the lowest position of the worshiper.
6: מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן בְּכֹהֲנָיו וּשְׁמוּאֵל בְּקֹרְאֵי שְׁמוֹ (mōsheh wĕʾahărōn bĕkhōhănāyw ûshmûʾêl bĕqōrĕʾê shĕmô, "Moses and Aaron were among his priests; Samuel was among those who called on his name!") — a remarkable trio: Moses (the lawgiver and mediator), Aaron (the first high priest), Samuel (the judge who anointed the first two kings). All three are intercessors — those who stand between God and people in the covenant relationship. Samuel was a Levite (1 Chronicles 6:28,33) though not of the Aaronic line — his association with Moses and Aaron elevates him to the ranks of the great mediators. קֹרִאים אֶל-יְהוָה וְהוּא יַעֲנֵם (qōrĕʾîm ʾel-yĕhwāh wĕhûʾ yaʿănêm, "They cried to the LORD and he answered them!") — the intercessors are defined by their prayer-life: crying to God and receiving answer.
7: בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן יְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם (bĕʿammûd ʿānān yĕdabbêr ʾălêhem, "He spoke to them from the pillar of cloud!") — the divine communication medium: the cloud-pillar of the wilderness (Exodus 33:9-10: "the pillar of cloud would descend and stand at the entrance of the tent"; Numbers 12:5: "the LORD came down in the pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the tent and called Aaron and Miriam"). שָׁמְרוּ עֵדֹתָיו וְחֹק נָתַן-לָמוֹ (shāmĕrû ʿêdōtāyw wĕḥōq nātan-lāmô, "They kept his testimonies and the law he gave them!") — ʿêdōtāyw: his testimonies/decrees (the covenant stipulations); ḥōq: the fixed ordinance. The intercessors were not merely conduits of divine speech but themselves obedient to what was given through them.
8: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ אַתָּה עֲנִיתָם אֵל נֹשֵׂא הָיִיתָ לָהֶם וְנֹקֵם עַל-עֲלִילוֹתָם (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhênû ʾattāh ʿănîtām ʾêl nōśêʾ hāyîtā lāhem wĕnōqêm ʿal-ʿălîlôtām, "O LORD our God, you answered them; you were a God who forgave them, yet you punished their offenses!") — the most theologically dense verse of the psalm. Nōśêʾ: one who lifts/forgives (bearing away iniquity — Exodus 34:7: "forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin"). Wĕnōqêm ʿal-ʿălîlôtām: "yet punishing/taking vengeance on their offenses" (nāqam: to avenge, punish; ʿălîlāh: deed, practice — usually the negative deeds). The unresolved tension: God both forgave and punished the same intercessors (Aaron at the golden calf — Exod 32; Moses at Meribah — Numbers 20:12). Forgiveness and accountability coexist without the one erasing the other — neither cheap grace nor pure retribution.
9: רוֹמְמוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ וְהִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְהַר קָדְשׁוֹ כִּי-קָדוֹשׁ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ (rômĕmû yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhênû wĕhishtaḥăwû lĕhar qōdshô kî-qādôsh yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhênû, "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy!") — the third and final refrain, now expanded and with a kî-clause giving the reason: because he is holy. The refrain has shifted: v3 "holy is he"; v5 "holy is he"; v9 "holy is the LORD our God" — the final refrain makes explicit what the pronoun had implied. The prostration shifts from "his footstool" (v5) to "his holy mountain" (Zion — Psalms 2:6; Psalms 15:1; Psalms 43:3) — movement toward the culminating place of the divine presence.
